hero_summary: Mustaar is a maqam of the Sikah family, yet it is not the same as Sikah itself. Its defining feature is that it begins with Jins Mustaar on Sikah (E half-flat), then activates Nahawand on Nawa (G).
short_identity: A Sikah-family maqam whose lower jins differs from ordinary Sikah by raising the second degree so that it leads toward Nahawand on the third level.

quick_facts.character: A subtle Sikah branch defined by its pull toward Nahawand in the upper region
quick_facts.common_start: It begins from Jins Mustaar on Sikah, then rises until Nahawand on Nawa becomes clear

sayr.summary: Mustaar belongs clearly to the Sikah family. Its particular identity comes from starting with Jins Mustaar on Sikah (E half-flat), then activating Jins Nahawand on Nawa (G), while Rast may appear above on Kardan (C in the upper octave). Modern analytical literature describes it as relatively archaic or old, and less common today as a full independent maqam, while Jins Mustaar itself remains active inside other Sikah-family contexts.
resting_tones: Sikah (E half-flat): the tonic and principal point of rest
Nawa (G): the crucial upper resting tone because it carries Jins Nahawand
Kardan (C in the upper octave): an upper resting area where Rast may appear
motion_notes: Its sayr is best understood as a move from Jins Mustaar at the tonic into Nahawand on Nawa, then optionally toward Rast above before returning to the tonic
Its key feature is that the second degree inside the lower jins is raised so that it behaves as a leading tone toward the Nahawand tonic on the third level
It should not be reduced to ‘Sikah plus Nahawand’ in a simplistic way, because the lower jins itself is what prepares and activates the upper Nahawand
If the difference between Jins Mustaar and Jins Sikah is not made audible, the performance may collapse into ordinary Sikah or into a passing modulation rather than a full maqam identity
common_path: Establish the tonic on Sikah (E half-flat)
Show the special color of Jins Mustaar below
Optionally expand toward Rast above, then return to Sikah

common_modulations: Basic Sikah usually highlights a different middle structure, whereas Mustaar is defined by activating Nahawand on the third level from within its lower jins
Huzam tends to highlight Hijaz in the upper or middle region, while Mustaar highlights Nahawand instead; this is a direct structural and auditory distinction
Iraq also belongs to the Sikah family, but it resolves on Iraq (B half-flat) rather than on Sikah (E half-flat), whereas Mustaar remains Sikah-based in its tonic

examples.title: Ya qalbi ta‘shaq leh
examples.note: Amar describes this dawr explicitly as being in Maqam Mustaar

reference_recordings.title: Ya qalbi ta‘shaq leh
reference_recordings.note: A documented example of Mustaar as an independent maqam
